proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, the E-phase ferroelectricity is intrinsically stable but is suppressed or preempted by competing magnetic and orbital orders, which can be controlled via epitaxial strain, layer stacking, and chemical doping.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U) and many-body computational techniques. The electronic structure of La5Ni2O12 and related m-layered nickelates will be calculated to map the energy landscape. We will then use exact diagonalization and variational Monte Carlo methods on effective multi-orbital Hubbard models, derived from Wannier-projected DFT, to compute magnetic and orbital correlation functions. The stability of the pure E-phase will be tested by selectively suppressing competing order parameters (e.g., by applying symmetry-breaking fields) in the simulations and observing the evolution of the ferroelectric polarization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative phase diagram showing the interplay between the E-phase and competing orders in a controlled theoretical setting. It will identify specific material parameters (strain, layer number, doping) that favor an isolated E-phase, providing clear targets for experimental synthesis. This will advance the fundamental understanding of multiferroicity in strongly correlated layered oxides.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified the E-phase in the presence of other orders, a systematic computational investigation to isolate and stabilize it by deliberately suppressing its competitors in nickelate systems has not been performed. The approach integrates modern electronic structure methods with advanced many-body techniques specifically tailored for layered correlated materials.

proposals.title: Impact of Structural Imperfections on Electronic Polarization and Competing Orders in Correlated Nickelate Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: In correlated oxide heterostructures, such as Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, novel electronic phases like unconventional superconductivity and magnetism are highly sensitive to lattice structure. Pre-2026 work has established that orthorhombic symmetry can stabilize specific magnetic and pairing tendencies, but real materials always contain small distortions, strain gradients, and point defects. The role of these ubiquitous imperfections in modifying the electronic polarization—a key driver of orbital order and emergent phases—remains poorly quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small orthorhombic distortions and impurity-induced disorder act as effective fields that pin, suppress, or reorient the electronic polarization, thereby altering the balance between competing superconducting and magnetic correlations in layered nickelates.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U) and many-body computational techniques, specifically dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT), to model pristine and distorted structures. The electronic polarization will be calculated using the modern theory of polarization (Berry phase). Controlled structural perturbations (e.g., oxygen octahedral rotations, cation substitutions, and biaxial strain) will be introduced. We will then use exact diagonalization and random phase approximation (RPA) methods on effective multi-orbital Hubbard models, derived from the DFT inputs, to compute the evolution of magnetic susceptibilities and superconducting pairing vertices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, microscopic map linking specific structural imperfections to changes in electronic polarization and the stability of emergent phases. It will deliver design principles for stabilizing desired quantum states in engineered heterostructures and clarify the intrinsic robustness of proposed pairing mechanisms in nickelates against disorder.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies often assume ideal symmetry, this work systematically quantifies how realistic, small-scale structural disorder—distinct from major symmetry breaking—couples to the electronic polarization and influences the delicate competition between orders in a multi-orbital setting.

proposals.title: Microscopic Mechanism of Magnetoelectric Coupling in the E-phase of Multiferroic Nickelates: A First-Principles and Model Hamiltonian Study
proposals.motivation: The discovery of the E-phase in layered nickelates, such as La5Ni2O6, presents a unique platform for multiferroicity, where magnetic order potentially induces ferroelectric polarization. However, the precise microscopic mechanism coupling the non-collinear spin order to specific lattice distortions remains unclear. Understanding this coupling is crucial for designing new magnetoelectric materials with strong cross-control of magnetic and electric properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the E-phase magnetic order, through spin-orbit coupling and exchange striction, drives a specific pattern of oxygen octahedral rotations and bond-length distortions, which is the primary source of the observed ferroelectric polarization.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined first-principles and model Hamiltonian approach. Density functional theory (DFT+U) calculations will be performed to map the total energy landscape as a function of relevant lattice distortion modes and spin configurations. The electronic structure and magnetic exchange parameters will be extracted to construct a spin-lattice Hamiltonian, which will be solved via classical Monte Carlo and exact diagonalization to study the coupled phase transitions. The Berry phase method will be used to compute the polarization from first principles for the relaxed structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, microscopic model for magnetoelectric coupling in the E-phase, identifying the dominant lattice modes and spin interactions responsible for polarization. It will provide predictive power for enhancing multiferroic responses in nickelates and related transition metal oxides through strain, doping, or heterostructuring.
proposals.novelty: While the E-phase is known, a detailed first-principles dissection of the spin-lattice coupling mechanism, specifically isolating the role of oxygen distortions versus cation displacements, has not been performed. Our integrated approach directly links the magnetic order parameter to the lattice-driven polarization.

proposals.title: Quantifying Spin-Lattice Coupling and its Role in Magnetic Ordering in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelates via Inelastic Neutron Scattering
proposals.motivation: In strongly correlated Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelates, the interplay between spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom is crucial for stabilizing novel magnetic and superconducting phases. Pre-2026 studies, including our own electronic structure and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, suggest strong spin-lattice coupling, but a direct, quantitative experimental measure of these interactions is lacking. Resolving the spin-phonon coupling is essential to understand the microscopic mechanisms driving phase competition and the suppression of long-range magnetic order in these materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific phonon modes, particularly those involving apical oxygen motions and Ni-O bond stretching, are strongly renormalized by magnetic correlations, and that this spin-lattice coupling is a primary driver of the magnetic phase diagram and the emergence of charge and spin density wave fluctuations in RP nickelates.
proposals.method: We will perform high-resolution inelastic neutron scattering (INS) on single crystals of the n=3 and n=5 RP nickelates (e.g., La4Ni3O10 and La6Ni5O16) to map the full phonon dispersion and magnetic excitation spectrum. Measurements will be conducted across key temperature transitions to track the evolution of phonon linewidths and energies with magnetic ordering. The data will be analyzed using linear spin-wave theory for the magnetic part and compared with lattice dynamical calculations from DFT+U to isolate the anomalous phonon contributions. The strength of the spin-phonon coupling will be quantified by fitting the observed renormalization using a model of coupled spin and lattice modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, momentum-resolved experimental map of spin-lattice coupling in the RP nickelates, quantifying its energy scale and identifying the specific lattice modes involved. It will establish a direct link between lattice dynamics and the magnetic phase stability, offering a crucial benchmark for theoretical models of correlation-driven phenomena in layered oxides. The results will inform strategies for manipulating magnetic and electronic phases via strain or interfacial engineering in oxide heterostructures.
proposals.novelty: While INS has been used to study magnetic excitations or phonons separately in correlated oxides, a combined, systematic study to directly quantify their mutual coupling in the context of the RP nickelate phase diagram is novel. This approach moves beyond indirect inferences from electronic structure calculations to provide definitive experimental evidence for the role of the lattice in mediating magnetic interactions in these systems.

proposals.title: First-Principles Determination of Ferroelectric Polarization and Its Coupling to Magnetic Order in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelate Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The discovery of superconductivity in infinite-layer and Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelates has opened a new frontier in correlated electron physics, but the role of structural distortions and potential polar order remains poorly understood. In thin-film heterostructures and layered bulk materials, subtle lattice distortions can induce ferroelectricity, which may couple strongly to magnetic and superconducting phases, offering a novel route for control. A quantitative, first-principles understanding of this polarization is a critical prerequisite for exploring such multiferroic or polar-superconducting phenomena in these nickelates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DFT+U calculations, by accurately capturing the correlated electronic structure and lattice dynamics of RP nickelates, will reveal a non-zero, layer-dependent ferroelectric polarization, and that this polarization is tunable by the number of NiO2 layers (m) and strongly coupled to the magnetic configuration of the nickel ions.
proposals.method: We will perform first-principles calculations using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with the PBEsol exchange-correlation functional. A Hubbard U correction, with values (U, J) benchmarked against known spectroscopic data for nickelates, will be applied to the Ni 3d states. The modern theory of polarization (Berry phase approach) will be implemented to compute the spontaneous polarization vector. We will systematically study the RP series (m=1-4) and relevant thin-film geometries, comparing paraelectric and distorted structures, and calculating the energy landscape for polarization switching under different antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic orderings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ab initio quantification of ferroelectric polarization in the superconducting nickelate family, establishing a materials-specific foundation. It will predict how polarization evolves with layer number and magnetic order, directly informing experimental searches for polar distortions and guiding the design of nickelate-based heterostructures for electric-field control of quantum phases.
proposals.novelty: While DFT+U is standard for nickelate magnetism, its application to compute the Berry-phase polarization in these materials is novel, moving beyond structural analysis to a direct quantum-mechanical calculation of an order parameter that may compete or cooperate with superconductivity.

proposals.title: Landau Theory for Coupled Magnetic and Polar Orders in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelate Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: The discovery of superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates like La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ under pressure has revitalized interest in their complex phase diagrams, which are expected to host intertwined magnetic and structural orders. Understanding the coupling between these orders is crucial for unraveling the pairing mechanism and controlling material properties, yet a systematic phenomenological framework for these coupled instabilities in layered nickelates is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the complex phase competition and emergent multiferroicity in layered nickelate heterostructures can be captured by a Landau free energy expansion with bilinear and biquadratic couplings between magnetic (e.g., antiferromagnetic) and polar (ferroelectric) order parameters, leading to distinct, tunable phase diagrams.
proposals.method: We will construct a symmetry-allowed Landau free energy functional, F(η, M), where η is a polar lattice distortion and M is the Néel vector. Coupling terms like λ η$_i$M$_j$M$_k$ (for improper ferroelectricity) and γ η$^2$M$^2$ will be included. The coefficients will be informed by first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U) calculations of relevant energy landscapes and phonon modes for prototype systems (e.g., La$_n$+1Ni$_n$O$_3n$+1 thin films). The resulting phase diagrams as a function of strain, doping, and interlayer coupling will be solved analytically and numerically.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified phenomenological model to interpret experimental phase transitions in nickelates and guide the design of heterostructures with coupled magnetic-ferroelectric functionality. It will establish a direct link between microscopic electronic structure calculations from our group and the macroscopic thermodynamic behavior of these correlated systems.
proposals.novelty: While Landau theory is standard, its specific application to the unique alternating single-layer/bilayer stacking and complex magnetic interactions of Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, informed by state-of-the-art DFT scattering calculations, is novel and addresses a key gap in the pre-2026 theoretical landscape for these materials.

proposals.title: Pairing Symmetry and Competing Orders in Doped Spin-Ladder Models: A Determinantal Quantum Monte Carlo Study
proposals.motivation: The discovery of superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates has revitalized interest in low-dimensional correlated systems as potential platforms for unconventional pairing. Doped two-leg ladder models are canonical minimal systems for studying the interplay between magnetic fluctuations, charge order, and superconductivity, offering crucial insights into the pairing mechanisms of layered cuprates and nickelates. A systematic, numerically exact study of pairing correlations in these models, especially with realistic multi-orbital interactions and doping levels, remains a critical pre-2026 challenge to bridge model Hamiltonians and real materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in doped multi-orbital Hubbard models on a two-leg ladder geometry, the dominant superconducting pairing symmetry and its strength are controlled by a delicate competition between nearest-neighbor superexchange, next-nearest-neighbor hopping, and orbital selectivity, leading to a crossover from d-wave-like to more exotic pairing states at specific doping levels.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale Determinantal Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations, specifically the Blankenbecler-Scalapino-Sugar (BSS) algorithm, to study two-leg ladder extensions of multi-orbital Hubbard models relevant to nickelates. The method will be implemented with an efficient GPU-accelerated codebase to handle the sign problem at moderate temperatures and interaction strengths. We will compute equal-time pairing correlation functions, spin and charge structure factors, and utilize stochastic analytic continuation to extract dynamical properties, systematically varying doping, inter-leg hopping, and orbital-dependent interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide numerically exact benchmarks for the pairing tendencies in a fundamental ladder geometry, directly informing the interpretation of experimental results in quasi-one-dimensional nickelate and cuprate analogs. It will establish a phase diagram detailing the competition between superconductivity, spin density waves, and charge order as a function of doping and model parameters, offering a quantitative link between simplified models and the layered superconductors studied in the researcher's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While DQMC has been applied to single-band ladders, its systematic application to multi-orbital ladder models with explicit computation of orbital-resolved pairing correlations is novel and directly targets the complex orbital physics highlighted in recent nickelate research, moving beyond effective single-band descriptions.

proposals.title: Coexistence and Competition of Three or More Electronic Phases in Layered Nickelate Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: The physics of strongly correlated oxides is often described by competing two-phase scenarios, such as antiferromagnetic insulator vs. paramagnetic metal. However, the complex phase diagrams of layered nickelates and related transition metal oxides, with multiple tuning parameters like doping, strain, and layer number, suggest the potential for richer multi-phase coexistence. Understanding such multi-phase landscapes is crucial for unraveling the mechanisms behind unconventional superconductivity and designing novel quantum states in heterostructures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in layered nickelate heterostructures under specific strain and doping conditions, three or more distinct electronic and magnetic phases—such as antiferromagnetic insulator, ferromagnetic metal, and superconducting—can stably coexist or compete on a microscopic scale, driven by competing interactions and spatial modulations.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of advanced computational techniques. First, we will use density functional theory (DFT) with Hubbard U corrections (DFT+U) and dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) to map the electronic structure of model layered nickelate systems. To probe multi-phase states, we will implement and apply real-space cluster extensions of DMFT and variational cluster approximations on large supercells, allowing for symmetry-breaking into multiple orders. The analysis will focus on layer-resolved magnetic susceptibilities, superconducting pairing tendencies, and charge density modulations to identify regions of multi-phase coexistence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical framework for understanding complex phase competition beyond the standard two-order paradigm. It will predict specific material platforms and experimental signatures (e.g., in scattering or local probes) for multi-phase coexistence, directly guiding future synthesis and characterization of nickelate heterostructures.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the ubiquitous two-phase model to systematically investigate the stability and properties of three or more coexisting phases represents a significant conceptual leap. The explicit focus on layered nickelates, where layer control offers a unique tuning knob, provides a novel and concrete material context for this exploration.

proposals.title: Electronic Homogeneity and Resilience Against Phase Separation in Correlated Nickelates and Cuprates
proposals.motivation: Electronic phase separation (EPS) is a common instability in strongly correlated systems like manganites and cuprates, often competing with or precluding uniform superconducting or magnetic phases. Understanding materials that robustly resist EPS is crucial for stabilizing and engineering homogeneous functional phases in oxides. This proposal addresses the inverse, less-explored problem: identifying the microscopic factors that enforce electronic uniformity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of orbital degeneracy, inter-layer coupling, and Fermi surface topology can suppress the long-range Coulomb interactions and local strain fields that drive EPS, leading to intrinsically homogeneous correlated phases.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of first-principles density functional theory (DFT) and many-body computational techniques. The electronic structure of candidate Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates (e.g., La$_n$NiO$_{n+1}$) and cuprates will be calculated via DFT+U. We will then use real-space dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) and variational cluster approximations to compute the charge compressibility and the stability of solutions against spatially inhomogeneous perturbations. The role of magnetic fluctuations and superconducting pairing tendencies will be assessed via random phase approximation (RPA) and functional renormalization group (fRG) methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a set of design principles for electronic homogeneity in correlated materials, directly informing the search for new superconductors. It will provide a quantitative, microscopic understanding of why certain layered oxides avoid phase separation, complementing the vast literature on systems that exhibit it.
proposals.novelty: While EPS has been extensively studied, a systematic search for materials and conditions that inherently suppress it is novel. The focus shifts from characterizing an instability to identifying the mechanisms of stability, a crucial but overlooked perspective for materials design.

proposals.title: Orbital-Selective Proximity Effects and Giant Electronic Response in Engineered Nickelate Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: Correlated oxides exhibit rich phase diagrams, but controlling their electronic and magnetic states with small external stimuli remains a challenge for next-generation sensors and low-power electronics. The discovery of superconductivity in infinite-layer and Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates has revealed a complex interplay of spin, charge, and orbital degrees of freedom, offering a promising platform for engineering amplified responses. However, a systematic design principle for achieving giant responses via targeted orbital-lattice coupling in heterostructures is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing heterostructures of alternating single-layer and bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate phases, we can create an orbital-selective proximity effect where the active e_g orbital manifold in one layer induces a giant, non-linear electronic response in the adjacent layer to small perturbations like strain or electric field.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U+SO) and many-body computational techniques, specifically single-site and cluster dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT), to model the electronic structure and magnetic correlations. We will design superlattices of La_n+1Ni_nO_3n+1 with alternating n=1 and n=2 blocks, varying the stacking periodicity. The response functions (spin and charge susceptibilities, orbital polarization) to applied biaxial strain and a static electric field will be calculated via linear response and constrained-DFT/DMFT methods. The analysis will focus on the scattering between Ni e_g (d_x2-y2, d_3z2-r2) orbitals across the interface as the amplification mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a theoretical framework for designing oxide heterostructures with orbital-engineered giant responses, directly informing synthesis efforts. It will provide specific predictions for novel quantum states in nickelate superlattices, such as strain-tuned magnetic transitions or field-induced metal-insulator transitions, expanding the phase diagram beyond bulk compounds. The results will advance the fundamental understanding of proximity effects in correlated, multi-orbital systems.
proposals.novelty: While proximity effects are studied in conventional superconductors, applying this concept to actively control the orbital degree of freedom in correlated nickelate heterostructures to achieve amplified responses is a novel and non-obvious design strategy. The focus on the alternating single-layer/bilayer motif, inspired by recent nickelate research, specifically targets a unique interfacial orbital configuration not present in homogeneous materials.

proposals.title: Mapping the Phase Competition Landscape in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelates via Layer-Controlled Quantum Fluctuations
proposals.motivation: The Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelates, such as the infinite-layer and La5Ni2O7 systems, exhibit a rich phase diagram with competing superconducting, magnetic, and insulating phases. Understanding the precise interplay of spin, charge, and orbital degrees of freedom across different layer numbers (m) is a central challenge in correlated electron physics. A systematic mapping of this competition is crucial for predicting and stabilizing novel quantum states, including unconventional superconductivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the systematic variation of the number of NiO2 layers (m) and inter-layer coupling in RP nickelates directly tunes the quantum fluctuations between competing orders, creating a universal phase competition map where specific layer sequences favor metallic, magnetic, or superconducting ground states.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of advanced computational techniques grounded in pre-2026 methods. First, we will use density functional theory (DFT) with dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) to calculate the electronic structure and local correlations for a series of RP nickelates (m=1 to 5). Second, we will perform large-scale determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) and density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations on effective multi-layer Hubbard models derived from the DFT+DMFT results. These will explicitly compute magnetic and pairing correlation functions to map the phase competition as a function of hopping parameters, layer stacking, and doping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive theoretical phase diagram for the RP nickelate series, identifying the specific material parameters and layer architectures that maximize superconducting pairing tendencies. It will establish general trends connecting structural motifs (single-layer–bilayer alternation) to dominant quantum fluctuations, offering concrete design principles for synthesizing new correlated oxide heterostructures with targeted properties.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined individual RP compounds, this proposal systematically connects the layer degree of freedom (m) to a quantitative map of competing quantum phases across a unified parameter space, explicitly treating the inter-layer magnetic and hopping processes that define the RP series.

proposals.title: Orbital-Selective Spin Transport and Control in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelate Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: Conventional spintronics primarily exploits spin-charge coupling, but the active lattice and orbital degrees of freedom in strongly correlated oxides remain an untapped resource for device functionality. The discovery of superconductivity in infinite-layer and Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates provides a new, complex materials platform where spin, charge, orbital, and lattice are intricately intertwined. Understanding how these degrees of freedom collectively govern spin-dependent transport is a critical step towards next-generation spintronic concepts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in layered nickelate heterostructures, the distinct orbital character and magnetic correlations of specific Ni-O layers can be engineered to create orbital-selective spin filters and highly efficient spin-orbit torque generation, mediated by coupling to lattice modes.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U+SO) and many-body computational techniques, specifically dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT), to calculate the layer- and orbital-resolved electronic structure and magnetic susceptibilities. We will implement a real-space Kubo formula approach to compute spin-dependent conductivities and spin Hall angles. Crucially, we will extend this framework to include electron-phonon coupling to specific Jahn-Teller and breathing modes to assess the role of the lattice in modulating spin transport.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design principles for 'orbital-spintronics' in correlated oxides, predicting novel transport signatures and identifying optimal nickelate layer stackings (e.g., single-layer vs. bilayer units) for spin manipulation. It will provide a theoretical foundation for interpreting future spin-pumping and spin-torque ferromagnetic resonance experiments on nickelate thin films.
proposals.novelty: While orbital physics is central to nickelate bulk properties, its explicit connection to spin transport mechanisms in engineered heterostructures is unexplored. The proposal uniquely integrates the active lattice degree of freedom, moving beyond a static electronic structure picture for spintronics.

proposals.title: Competing Superexchange and Double-Exchange in Layered Nickelates: A Route to Enhanced Superconductivity and Novel Magnetic Phases
proposals.motivation: In layered nickelates like the Ruddlesden-Popper series, the interplay between localized t2g spins and itinerant eg electrons is crucial for superconductivity and magnetism. Pre-2026 models often emphasize double-exchange via the eg channel, but the direct antiferromagnetic (AFM) superexchange between localized t2g cores on neighboring sites, a key ingredient in cuprates, has been underexplored. Introducing this direct superexchange competition is essential for a complete microscopic understanding of the magnetic phase diagram and its connection to superconducting pairing tendencies in these quantum materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including direct AFM superexchange between localized t2g spins will compete with ferromagnetic double-exchange, stabilizing a more robust AFM background that can enhance d-wave superconducting pairing correlations and potentially lead to novel magnetic phases in layered nickelate heterostructures.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-orbital Hubbard model for Ni d-orbitals, extending previous models by explicitly adding a direct AFM Heisenberg coupling (J) between t2g spins on adjacent sites. The model will be solved using advanced many-body techniques, primarily the determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) method where possible, and supplemented by density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations for quasi-1D systems and static mean-field theory for initial phase diagram mapping. We will compute spin-spin correlation functions, superconducting pairing susceptibilities, and single-particle spectral functions to track the evolution of magnetic and superconducting order with varying superexchange strength J and doping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework to understand the magnetic ground states and superconducting instabilities in nickelates by bridging concepts from cuprate (superexchange-dominated) and manganite (double-exchange-dominated) physics. It will yield a quantitative phase diagram predicting how t2g superexchange tunes between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic regimes, directly informing the interpretation of neutron scattering and resonant inelastic x-ray scattering (RIXS) experiments on these materials.
proposals.novelty: While superexchange is well-known, its explicit inclusion as a *competing* force to double-exchange in models of superconducting nickelates is a significant and non-trivial extension of pre-2026 theoretical approaches, which typically treat the t2g spins as a passive background or consider only Hund's coupling.

proposals.title: Electron-Doping Phase Diagram and Competing Orders in Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelate Superconductors
proposals.motivation: The discovery of superconductivity in hole-doped infinite-layer nickelates has established a new arena for unconventional superconductivity, but the phase diagram under electron doping remains largely unexplored. Given the profound electron-hole asymmetry observed in cuprates and iron-based superconductors, systematically inverting the doping regime in nickelates is crucial to test theoretical models, identify the dominant pairing glue, and uncover potential new correlated phases. This investigation is essential for a unified understanding of nickelate superconductivity and its relation to other high-Tc families.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that electron doping in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates will suppress the magnetic correlations dominant near half-filling and stabilize a distinct superconducting dome, potentially with a different pairing symmetry and lower critical temperature than the hole-doped side, due to fundamental asymmetries in the electronic structure and orbital character of the doped carriers.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of density functional theory (DFT) and dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) to calculate the electronic structure and magnetic susceptibilities for electron-doped La_n+1Ni_nO_3n+1 (n=1,2,3). We will then construct multi-orbital Hubbard models and analyze them using determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) and functional renormalization group (fRG) methods to map the phase diagram, focusing on the evolution of spin/charge fluctuations and the leading superconducting pairing tendencies as a function of electron doping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive theoretical phase diagram for electron-doped layered nickelates, identifying the nature of magnetic and charge orders and the dominant pairing channel. It will establish whether superconductivity is a generic feature of doped nickelates or specific to hole doping, thereby constraining microscopic mechanisms and guiding future experimental synthesis efforts.
proposals.novelty: While extensive pre-2026 work has focused on hole-doped nickelates, a systematic theoretical study of the electron-doped regime, particularly across the Ruddlesden-Popper series using advanced many-body techniques, is novel and addresses a critical open question in the field.

proposals.title: Quantum Spin Fluctuations and Pairing in Correlated Nickelate Heterostructures: A Dynamical Mean-Field Theory Study
proposals.motivation: In models of correlated oxide heterostructures, such as the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, localized t2g spins are often treated as classical Ising variables to simplify calculations. This approximation neglects crucial quantum spin fluctuations, which are known to mediate unconventional superconductivity in other contexts. A fully quantum treatment of these spins is essential to accurately capture magnetic correlations, spin dynamics, and their role in pairing tendencies within these complex layered materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating the t2g spins as quantum mechanical S=1/2 degrees of freedom will reveal enhanced low-energy spin fluctuations, significantly modify the magnetic phase diagram, and potentially enhance or alter the symmetry of superconducting pairing correlations compared to the classical spin approximation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-orbital Hubbard model for the nickelate layers, explicitly coupling itinerant eg electrons to localized quantum t2g spins via Hund's coupling. The problem will be solved using Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) with a continuous-time Quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) impurity solver capable of handling the full quantum spin bath. We will benchmark against classical spin (Ising) results and compute dynamical spin structure factors, pairing susceptibilities, and phase diagrams as a function of doping and layer stacking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic comparison of quantum versus classical spin treatments in layered nickelate models, quantifying the impact of quantum spin fluctuations on magnetic and superconducting instabilities. It will establish a more rigorous theoretical framework for interpreting scattering and spectroscopic data on these materials, directly informing the search for new quantum phases in oxide heterostructures.
proposals.novelty: While DMFT+CT-QMC is established, its application to layered nickelate models with explicit, fully quantum mechanical localized spins (beyond the static or classical approximation) is novel and addresses a key simplification in prior theoretical studies of these systems.

proposals.title: Real-Time Dynamics of Competing Magnetic Phases in Strongly Correlated Nickelate Thin Films and Heterostructures
proposals.motivation: In strongly correlated oxides like the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, the interplay between antiferromagnetic, ferromagnetic, and superconducting phases is central to their exotic properties. While static phase separation is well-documented, the kinetic pathways and growth laws governing the formation of ferromagnetic droplets within an antiferromagnetic matrix remain poorly understood. Elucidating these dynamics is crucial for controlling emergent phases in oxide heterostructures and thin films, which are promising for next-generation electronic devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the real-time kinetics of magnetic phase separation in nickelate systems is governed by a complex interplay of double-exchange ferromagnetic tendencies and superexchange antiferromagnetic correlations, leading to non-trivial domain growth laws and potentially self-organized patterns that influence superconducting pairing tendencies.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale real-space dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) and time-dependent Gutzwiller simulations on multi-orbital Hubbard models relevant to nickelates. The real-time evolution will be simulated using a non-equilibrium extension of the Lanczos-based impurity solver within DMFT, coupled with a Monte Carlo sampling of classical spin configurations to capture the growth of magnetic domains. This will be complemented by density functional theory (DFT) calculations to parameterize the electronic hopping and interaction terms for specific layered structures, such as the alternating single-layer–bilayer nickelates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed theoretical framework for the non-equilibrium kinetics of magnetic phase separation in nickelates, mapping out the timescales and morphological evolution of competing phases. It will establish a direct link between dynamic magnetic correlations and the stability of superconducting phases in these materials, offering new design principles for dynamically controlling quantum states in oxide heterostructures.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have focused on static phase diagrams, this proposal uniquely targets the *real-time dynamics* of phase separation in multi-orbital nickelate models, explicitly connecting kinetic processes to the established landscape of magnetic and superconducting correlations in Dagotto's recent work.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Transport and Magnetoresistance in Phase-Separated Nickelate Superlattices: A Real-Space Cluster Dynamical Mean-Field Study
proposals.motivation: Strongly correlated oxides, like the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates, often exhibit electronic phase separation (PS), where metallic and insulating regions coexist. Pre-2026, the focus has been on identifying PS phases and their magnetic pairing tendencies, but their direct impact on anisotropic transport and magnetoresistance—key for device functionality—remains poorly quantified. This gap limits our understanding of how nanoscale inhomogeneity governs macroscopic electronic properties in layered quantum materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the real-space configuration of phase-separated metallic and magnetic insulating domains in layered nickelates dictates strong anisotropy between in-plane and out-of-plane electrical conductivity and leads to a non-monotonic, tunable magnetoresistance sensitive to layer stacking and external field orientation.
proposals.method: We will employ real-space cluster dynamical mean-field theory (CDMFT) on multi-orbital Hubbard models for La5Ni2O7-type alternating layer-bilayer structures. The inhomogeneous PS state will be stabilized via inhomogeneous Hartree-Fock initialization. Electrical conductivity tensors and magnetoresistance will be calculated using the Kubo formula within CDMFT, implemented via continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo as the impurity solver. Magnetic field effects will be incorporated via Peierls phases and Zeeman coupling to study field-dependent scattering between coexisting phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed, microscopic calculation of anisotropic transport coefficients directly from a PS ground state in nickelates, linking nanoscale electronic texture to measurable galvanometric responses. It will establish design principles for tuning magnetoresistance in oxide heterostructures via layer engineering and phase competition.
proposals.novelty: While PS is known in these systems, a first-principles-based computation of its full anisotropic magnetotransport properties—moving beyond homogeneous effective medium approximations—is novel and non-trivial, as it requires capturing the interplay of hopping, scattering, and magnetic modes in a spatially resolved quantum many-body framework.

proposals.title: DMRG Study of Competing Magnetic and Pairing Correlations in Two-Dimensional Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelate Models
proposals.motivation: The discovery of superconductivity in infinite-layer nickelates and their Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) layered variants has opened a new frontier in correlated electron physics, challenging the cuprate paradigm. A key unresolved question is the nature of the magnetic background and its connection to superconducting pairing tendencies in the two-dimensional NiO2 planes, which is difficult to address with conventional quantum Monte Carlo due to potential sign problems. Understanding this competition is crucial for identifying the pairing mechanism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that advanced 2D DMRG simulations on multi-orbital Hubbard models for RP nickelates will reveal robust antiferromagnetic or stripe magnetic correlations that coexist with or are proximate to strong d-wave superconducting pairing fluctuations, with the balance controlled by doping and the number of stacked layers.
proposals.method: We will employ state-of-the-art density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) techniques for two-dimensional systems, specifically using large cylindrical geometries with careful finite-width scaling. The study will be based on a two-orbital (Ni 3dx2-y2 and 3dz2) Hubbard model with realistic hopping parameters and Coulomb interactions derived from prior electronic structure calculations for La3Ni2O7 and La5Ni2O8. We will utilize the ITensor library, implementing non-Abelian symmetries to improve efficiency and targeting both spin-spin and pair-pair correlation functions to map the phase diagram.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-precision, numerically exact characterization of the ground state magnetic order and superconducting pairing susceptibilities in a realistic 2D model for layered nickelates. It will establish a direct, quantitative link between the electronic structure of specific RP phases and their emergent quantum many-body phenomena, guiding future experimental searches for enhanced superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: While DMRG has been applied to 2D cuprate models, its systematic application to the multi-orbital, layer-resolved physics of nickelate thin films and heterostructures represents a novel and non-trivial extension, directly addressing the complex interplay between magnetism and pairing in this new material family.

proposals.title: Magnetic and Orbital Correlations in a Multi-Orbital e_g Model for Manganite Heterostructures: A Computational Study
proposals.motivation: The physics of manganite thin films and heterostructures, known for colossal magnetoresistance and complex orbital ordering, is often modeled using simplified single-orbital or Ising-like orbital descriptions. However, the full degeneracy and spatial anisotropy of the Mn e_g (d_x^2-y^2 and d_3z^2-r^2) orbitals are crucial for capturing correct magnetic exchange pathways, Jahn-Teller distortions, and the interplay between spin, charge, and orbital degrees of freedom. A systematic study using a full two-orbital degenerate model is needed to establish the generic phase diagram and emergent excitations in layered geometries relevant to interface engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating the full e_g orbital degeneracy and direct inter-orbital hopping in a bilayer/square lattice Hubbard model will stabilize novel magnetic and orbital-ordered phases not captured by single-orbital models, and will significantly modify the spectrum of magnetic and orbital excitations, including the potential for composite spin-orbital modes.
proposals.method: We will construct a two-orbital Hubbard model for e_g electrons on a layered lattice, including realistic hopping integrals, Hund's coupling, on-site Coulomb repulsion, and crystal field splittings. The model will be solved using state-of-the-art computational techniques from Prof. Dagotto's toolkit, primarily determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) for finite-temperature properties and the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) for ground state correlations in quasi-1D geometries. We will compute spin, orbital, and combined correlation functions, as well as the relevant structure factors and single-particle spectral functions to map the phase diagram as a function of doping, interlayer coupling, and interaction strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational phase diagram for the two-orbital e_g system in a layered geometry, directly informing the interpretation of experiments on manganite superlattices. It will quantify the stability of known orbital-ordered states (like the A-type antiferromagnet) and predict new competing phases, while characterizing the nature of low-energy magnetic and orbital excitations that could be probed by neutron scattering or resonant inelastic x-ray scattering (RIXS).
proposals.novelty: While multi-orbital models are common for t_2g systems (e.g., ruthenates), a comprehensive computational study focusing on the distinct physics of the spatially extended and degenerate e_g manifold in a controlled, layered lattice setting is lacking. The explicit treatment of inter-orbital hopping and the search for composite spin-orbital collective modes beyond the standard spin-wave theory constitutes a significant advance.

The Hamiltonian includes terms for long-range dipolar interactions, local soft-mode dynamics, and random-site potentials derived from DFT calculations of different cation configurations. Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations using this model can then statistically sample the vast configuration space to predict emergent properties, such as composition-dependent Curie temperatures and dielectric tunability, directly linking atomic-scale disorder to macroscopic functional behavior.

A high-throughput computational pipeline then screens known and hypothetical layered cuprates and related materials against these criteria to generate a shortlist of high-probability candidates. Synthesis efforts are directed exclusively at these predicted materials, with subsequent characterization of their superconducting properties validating or refining the theoretical model. This creates a targeted, iterative discovery cycle that replaces random structural searching with a physics-based design rule.

ideas.title: Engineered Orbital Symmetry Breaking for Tunable Finite-q Superconductivity
ideas.description: This redesigned idea proposes to engineer the conditions for a finite-wavevector (q≠0) superconducting condensate by introducing a spatially periodic, non-magnetic potential through substrate patterning or controlled strain in thin-film heterostructures. The core mechanism is an orbital coupling that modifies the kinetic energy of Cooper pairs without suppressing spin-singlet pairing, explicitly breaking translational symmetry to favor a condensate with a matching finite momentum. This approach transforms the search from vague parameter space exploration into a targeted materials design and experimental protocol, where the period and strength of the engineered potential are direct control parameters. The investigation will combine Ginzburg-Landau modeling of the modulated state with specific proposals for spectroscopic (STM) and transport validation in fabricated superconducting monolayers on patterned substrates.

ideas.title: Inducing Macroscopic Phase Separation via Intrinsic Geometric Frustration
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that macroscopic phase separation in materials is always an extrinsic phenomenon driven by factors like slow quenching or applied fields. By drawing an analogy to geometrically frustrated spin systems, where intrinsic lattice geometry leads to macroscopic degeneracy and long-range order via 'order by disorder,' we propose to find intrinsic material conditions for macroscopic separation. The adapted research idea is to design or identify material systems (e.g., block copolymers with specific chain architectures or alloys with particular lattice symmetries) where the local energetic preferences for phase organization are geometrically frustrated. This intrinsic frustration should prevent the system from settling into a nanoscale morphologies and instead force a resolution through macroscopic domain formation, driven by thermal fluctuations or quantum zero-point motion. The goal is to theoretically model and experimentally realize such systems to demonstrate that macroscopic separation can be an intrinsic, equilibrium outcome.

ideas.description: This research direction proposes to isolate and study a single ferromagnetic nanocluster embedded in an insulating matrix, but with the explicit goal of using its localized electronic and magnetic properties as a sensitive probe for the three-dimensional coupling field exerted upon it. By combining single-entity measurement techniques (e.g., single-cluster spectroscopy, magnetic force microscopy on identified clusters) with theoretical models that include longer-range interplane and intralayer couplings from the surrounding matrix, the work will decode how these extended weak interactions collectively determine the cluster's anisotropy, resonance behavior, and switching dynamics. This reveals the effective magnetic environment 'seen' by a solitary nanocluster, bridging the gap between atomically precise single-object studies and the realistic, distributed interactions present in functional nanostructured materials.

ideas.title: Dynamical Decoupling via Emergent Synchronization in Disordered Quantum Magnets
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that static stripe order is a prerequisite for dynamical decoupling in quantum materials like cuprates. Drawing an analogy from neuroscience's 'binding problem', where coherent perception emerges from transient synchronization of distributed neural populations without a fixed anatomical structure, we adapt this solution to condensed matter physics. The adapted research idea investigates whether dynamical decoupling can occur in the absence of static stripe order through emergent, self-organized synchronization of spins. By modeling disordered or glassy quantum magnetic systems with competing interactions, the research will search for regimes where the dynamics themselves generate a transient, correlated state that facilitates decoupled spin precession, effectively creating order from disorder on a dynamical timescale. This approach shifts the focus from searching for static spatial order to identifying the conditions for dynamic self-organization that enables coherent quantum behavior.

ideas.title: Charge Order Doubling as a Homogeneous Nanoscale Driver for CMR Beyond Percolation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates systems where the charge order period is double that of the superconducting pair density wave, creating a locked, commensurate nanoscale pattern. This intrinsic, homogeneous electronic texture—comprising alternating regions of enhanced and suppressed pair coherence—is proposed as a novel scattering landscape for charge carriers. An applied magnetic field is hypothesized to dramatically alter quantum interference and scattering cross-sections within this texture, leading to CMR without requiring macroscopic phase separation or percolation. The core synergy is using a specific intertwined order parameter relationship to engineer a homogeneous alternative to the phase-separated percolative scenario traditionally associated with CMR.

ideas.description: This study moves beyond a generic parameter scan by first using group theory to catalog all symmetry-allowed longer-range interplane couplings (e.g., next-nearest neighbor, next-next-nearest neighbor) within the specific crystal structure of the material system. The relevance of each coupling term is then assessed theoretically using a scaling analysis near the hypothesized decoupling critical point. This principled framework directs computational and experimental efforts exclusively toward measuring or simulating the subset of couplings predicted to be most relevant, yielding fundamental insight into the stability and universality class of the decoupling transition.

It employs a correlated microscopy approach, pairing atomic-resolution chemical and strain mapping with nanoscale scanning tunneling spectroscopy on the same sample regions. A statistical analysis framework cross-correlates the cation distribution maps with the electronic landscape measurements. This allows for the direct quantification of how much of the observed potential disorder is attributable to cation identity versus secondary effects like local strain or oxygen vacancy clustering.

ideas.title: Dynamical Decoupling and Percolation in a Nanoscale Phase-Separated Josephson Junction Array
ideas.description: This research develops a unified transport model for a system in a homogeneous nanoscale phase-separated state, treating it as a disordered array of Josephson-coupled puddles. The model quantifies how the global percolation threshold for charge transport correlates with the onset of dynamical decoupling between layers, where the effective Josephson coupling vanishes. This synergy allows the prediction of quantum coherence thresholds based on the statistical geometry of the percolating network, and conversely, uses transport signatures of dynamical decoupling to probe the underlying nanoscale morphology. The combined framework provides a novel pathway to engineer quantum coherent states by controlling percolation in geometrically frustrated or driven systems.

ideas.title: Probe spin glass state in homogeneous phase separation via polymer physics-inspired scattering techniques
ideas.description: This research idea investigates the detailed relationship between the spin glass state and homogeneous short-ranged charge-orbital order by drawing an analogy from polymer physics. In soft matter, multi-speckle dynamic light scattering and X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy are used to resolve the slow, heterogeneous dynamics of gel states within structurally homogeneous polymer solutions. Adapting this solution, we propose using coherent X-ray (XPCS) or neutron scattering techniques to measure the spatiotemporal correlation of fluctuations in a condensed matter system with short-ranged charge-orbital order. By analyzing the dynamical heterogeneity and decay times of these correlations, the experiment can directly probe whether the spin glass state originates from the gradual freezing of these local orbital dynamics, thereby elucidating if the spin glass is an intrinsic part of the homogeneous phase's thermodynamics or a distinct kinetic arrest phenomenon.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Energy Landscape Methods to Cation Disorder
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from computational biophysics, specifically the study of protein folding energy landscapes. In that field, the conformational energy surface is highly disordered and rugged, analogous to the random potential created by a solid solution of different A-site cations in a perovskite. The analogous solution involves specialized sampling algorithms (e.g., funnel metadynamics, basin-hopping) designed to efficiently explore such complex landscapes and find low-energy states. Adapted to this materials science context, these computational methods can be used to model the disordered alloy's potential. By treating the arrangement of different A-site cations as creating a funneled but rough energy landscape, these techniques can sample cation configurations more effectively than standard methods, identifying probable short-range order, metastable states, and directly linking specific local cation environments to changes in the electronic structure and functional properties.

ideas.title: Engineering 3D Long-Range Order in Nanocomposites via Controlled Interplane Coupling
ideas.description: This research direction aims to synthesize self-assembled or templated nanocomposite materials with true three-dimensional long-range order in their phase-separated nanodomains. Crucially, the design and characterization will explicitly account for and engineer longer-range interplane and inter-domain couplings (e.g., elastic, dipolar, or electronic) beyond nearest neighbors. The synergy lies in using these engineered 3D coupling effects as the driving force to achieve and stabilize the long-range structural order, while simultaneously using the resulting ordered architecture to probe and exploit novel correlated states that depend on specific, non-local interaction pathways. This moves beyond creating mere structural order to creating functionally pre-programmed matter where spatial and coupling order are co-designed.

ideas.title: Applying Pattern Formation Principles to Stabilize Non-Zero Momentum Superconductivity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a non-zero wavevector (finite-momentum) superconducting condensate requires a highly specific, fine-tuned set of circumstances (e.g., precise spin-orbit coupling and magnetic field alignment). By analogy to pattern formation in nonlinear fluid dynamics, where periodic structures are stabilized by nonlinear interactions and boundary effects, we propose investigating whether similar principles can broaden the stability domain for finite-q condensates. The adapted research idea involves treating the finite-momentum order parameter as a spatially periodic pattern and searching for 'pattern-selecting' mechanisms in condensed matter systems. This could include designing sample geometries with tailored boundaries or interfaces, introducing deliberate spatial modulations in the pairing potential, or leveraging the nonlinear competition with other orders (e.g., charge density waves) to selectively stabilize and pin a condensate at a desired, non-zero wavevector under more general, experimentally achievable conditions.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of A-Site Disorder via Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse design methodology from computational drug discovery and protein engineering to solve the materials inverse problem of designing A-site cation disorder. In the analogous field, target protein functions are specified, and evolutionary algorithms search chemical space for candidate molecules. Here, we specify a target colossal magnetoresistance (CMR) profile—defined by metrics like transition temperature (T_C), resistivity peak width, and magnetoresistance magnitude—as a multi-objective fitness function. We then employ an evolutionary optimization algorithm to explore the vast composition space of A-site cation mixtures (e.g., varying ratios of La, Pr, Ca, Sr, Ba, etc.). The search is guided by a computationally efficient surrogate model, such as a coarse-grained Hamiltonian incorporating key interactions (ionic size mismatch, local strain, Coulomb interactions, and double exchange strength) that govern nanoscale phase separation. The output is a set of proposed A-site disordered perovskite compositions predicted to achieve the target CMR behavior, effectively inverting the traditional structure-property discovery process.

ideas.title: Phase-sensitive nanoscale imaging of pair density waves in thin-film stripe-ordered superconductors
ideas.description: This research direction combines the goal of detecting a pair density wave (PDW) order parameter with the unique environment of thin-film complex oxides. It proposes to fabricate thin films of stripe-ordered superconductors (like LBCO) where epitaxial strain and dimensionality can stabilize and enhance the PDW state. Using phase-sensitive nanoscale probes (e.g., scanning SQUID, Josephson scanning tunneling microscopy) on these engineered films, one can directly image the spatial modulations of the superconducting phase and amplitude, correlating them with the film's inherent nanoscale phase-separated electronic texture. This approach creates a tunable platform to isolate the PDW signature from competing orders and test its response to strain, thickness, and disorder.

ideas.title: Adapting Long-Range Coupling Analysis from Protein Folding to Layered Materials
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational biology—specifically, the use of coarse-grained elastic network models (ENMs) like the Anisotropic Network Model to study long-range interactions in protein folding—to the condensed matter problem of interplane coupling in layered materials. In protein studies, ENMs consider harmonic couplings between all residues within a cutoff to analyze stability and allostery, moving beyond nearest-neighbor approximations. The adapted idea applies this network approach by modeling a layered material as a 3D network where nodes represent planes or lattice sites and springs represent couplings (exchange, hopping, or elastic). By constructing and solving this model, one can systematically probe the impact of longer-range interplane and diagonal couplings on the proposed decoupling mechanism. The analysis of the network's eigenmodes and response to localized perturbations will reveal how decoupling propagates through the structure and identify which specific beyond-nearest-neighbor couplings are most influential, providing a quantitative framework to challenge the assumption of limited coupling range.

ideas.title: Characterize random potential from A-site cation disorder via scanning probe noise spectroscopy
ideas.description: This idea proposes to characterize the uniformity of the random potential induced by A-site cation solid solution by adapting a solution from cosmology. In cosmology, the statistical properties of primordial density fluctuations are inferred from the power spectrum of the cosmic microwave background, a fossil record of those fluctuations. Analogously, in a disordered perovskite or similar material, direct atomic-resolution mapping of every A-site cation may be impractical. The adapted solution is to use scanning probe microscopy (STM/c-AFM) to acquire a high-resolution spatial map of local electronic properties (e.g., tunneling current, surface potential), which are perturbed by the underlying cation disorder. A statistical analysis of this map—calculating its power spectral density or autocorrelation function—will then be used to extract quantitative parameters (e.g., correlation length, variance, spectral exponent) that characterize the amplitude and spatial uniformity of the random potential field, providing a direct experimental measure of the disorder's impact.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Engineering's Directed Evolution to Design Layered Materials with Targeted Interlayer Decoupling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'directed evolution' methodology from protein engineering to the search for layered materials with vanishing interlayer coupling. In the analogous field, desired protein functions are discovered by generating large combinatorial libraries and applying high-throughput functional screens, iterating on the best candidates. The adapted research idea applies this solution to materials science by synthesizing combinatorial libraries of layered cuprate-like materials, where the identity, thickness, and stacking order of spacer layers between superconducting planes are systematically varied. These libraries are then rapidly screened using spatially-resolved probes sensitive to interlayer phase coherence (e.g., a scanning SQUID microscope mapping the c-axis Josephson effect). This high-throughput, function-driven search strategy aims to efficiently navigate the vast chemical and structural space of layered materials to identify novel compositions and architectures that inherently enforce the decoupling of superconducting planes, a key requirement for certain theoretical proposals regarding high-temperature superconductivity.

ideas.description: This research direction combines the intentional introduction of controlled extrinsic defects with high-resolution scanning probe microscopy to actively manipulate and image the nanoscale phase separation in colossal magnetoresistance (CMR) materials. By using techniques like scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) or conductive atomic force microscopy (c-AFM), we will not only image the resulting phase domains but also use the probe tip to inject or reposition defects in situ, directly testing their role as nucleation sites or pinning centers for phase boundaries. This allows for the creation of a causal map between specific defect configurations, the resulting phase landscape, and the macroscopic transport properties, moving beyond passive observation to active phase domain engineering.

ideas.title: Epitaxial Strain Engineering of Long-Range Ordered Nanocomposite Superlattices
ideas.description: This research direction combines epitaxial strain control of phase separation with the goal of long-range order to create coherent, substrate-defined nanocomposite superlattices. By depositing a phase-separating material system onto a carefully selected single-crystal substrate, the anisotropic misfit strain will be used to both set the characteristic domain size and dictate the spatial registry of the domains. This approach forces the self-assembled nanostructure to nucleate and grow in epitaxial alignment with the substrate's crystal lattice, transforming a statistically isotropic phase-separated morphology into a deterministic, long-range ordered array. The resulting material would be a single-crystal thin film hosting a periodic nanocomposite structure, offering unprecedented control over functional properties like correlated electron behavior or phonon transport.

ideas.title: Mechanism-driven search for defect-mediated intrinsic macroscopic phase separation
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that specific classes of intrinsic topological defects can provide the long-range strain fields necessary to drive phase separation beyond the nanoscale. The approach focuses on material systems like block copolymers with liquid crystalline segments or correlated electron systems where theory suggests defects can couple strongly to order parameters. A combined computational and high-throughput experimental pipeline will systematically introduce and characterize defect arrays, measuring their direct impact on domain coarsening dynamics. The core advance is a mechanism-focused framework that yields falsifiable predictions, transforming an open-ended search into a targeted investigation of a plausible intrinsic driver for macroscopic separation.

ideas.title: Direct 3D Nanoscale Tomography of Long-Range Coupling in Phase-Decoupled Materials
ideas.description: This research direction combines semiconductor-derived scanning probe microscopy (e.g., scanning tunneling microscopy, piezoresponse force microscopy) with advanced tomographic reconstruction techniques to image the nanoscale phase domains in three dimensions. Crucially, it applies this high-resolution 3D imaging to directly measure how longer-range interplane couplings—extending beyond nearest neighbors—manifest as spatially extended strain fields and electronic potential gradients that connect seemingly decoupled domains. This allows for the experimental validation of theoretical coupling models by correlating specific 3D domain architectures with their measured functional responses, thereby uncovering the precise nanoscale pathways through which long-range interactions either stabilize or disrupt macroscopic phase coherence.

ideas.title: Mapping Correlated Disorder in Perovskites via Geophysical Seismic Tomography Methods
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of a uniform random potential in systems with A-site cation disorder. By drawing an analogy from geophysics, where seismic tomography reconstructs correlated subsurface structures from seemingly random seismic events, we propose an adapted experimental-computational method. In perovskites, instead of assuming uncorrelated disorder, we treat the material as an 'unknown interior' to be mapped. Using a battery of local spectroscopic techniques applied at multiple points across a sample, we collect a dataset of local chemical, electronic, and vibrational responses. Advanced inversion algorithms, inspired by seismic tomography, are then employed to reconstruct a spatial map of the effective random potential energy landscape imposed by the cation arrangement. This approach directly tests for and quantifies correlations or non-uniformity in the disorder, moving beyond statistical averages to visualize its real-space structure and impact on electronic or ionic transport.

ideas.title: Microscopic Causality and Noise Spectroscopy of Dynamical Layer Decoupling
ideas.description: This redesigned project establishes a causal mechanism for dynamical layer decoupling by combining real-time, non-local transport and shot noise measurements with independent layer control. A dual-gated device architecture allows for the independent tuning of disorder and carrier density in each layer while simultaneously applying inter-layer bias. The core innovation is using current noise spectroscopy to directly measure the statistics and timescales of charge transfer between layers, moving beyond an assumed Josephson coupling to directly probe the emergent inter-layer conductance. This approach distinguishes whether decoupling stems from many-body localization, phase fluctuations, or a competing ordered state by correlating noise signatures with layer-selective spectroscopic measurements of the electronic state.

ideas.title: Direct Imaging and Percolation Modeling of Dynamic Charge Transport Pathways
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-resolution scanning probe microscopy (e.g., conductive AFM, Kelvin probe force microscopy) with advanced percolation theory to directly visualize and model the formation of conductive pathways in nanoscale cluster systems. By applying semiconductor imaging techniques, we can map the evolving spatial distribution of conductive and insulating domains under varying bias, temperature, or chemical potential. These real-space, time-resolved images provide the exact network topology and cluster statistics needed to parameterize and validate predictive percolation models of charge transport. This synergy creates a powerful feedback loop where imaging data refines the physical models, and the models, in turn, guide targeted imaging experiments to probe critical transitions and optimize material design for enhanced conductivity.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryo-EM's Single-Particle Analysis for EXAFS to Map Heterogeneous Local Order
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core computational strategy of single-particle cryo-EM to EXAFS spectroscopy for local structural analysis. In cryo-EM, heterogeneous mixtures of protein conformations are resolved by collecting vast datasets of 2D projections and using computational sorting to reconstruct distinct 3D states. The analogous problem in condensed matter physics is that bulk EXAFS often provides only an average local structure, obscuring microscopically heterogeneous charge-orbital order. The adapted solution is to treat a macroscopic EXAFS measurement as an ensemble average over a vast number of local probes (X-ray absorption events). By applying advanced machine learning classification and clustering algorithms to large EXAFS datasets—potentially collected with high spatial or temporal resolution—one can computationally 'sort' the spectra into groups representing distinct local structural states. This would allow for the statistical mapping and quantitative analysis of co-existing short-ranged ordered phases, defect configurations, and dynamic local fluctuations in complex quantum materials, moving beyond the traditional average structural picture.

ideas.title: Dynamic control of phase domain scaling via piezoelectric strain tuning
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the static limitation of traditional epitaxial strain by integrating the functional thin film onto a ferroelectric piezoelectric substrate. Applying a voltage to the substrate induces a tunable, biaxial strain in the epitaxial film, enabling real-time and reversible control of the strain state. This allows for the continuous mapping of the characteristic phase separation length scale as a function of strain on a single sample, dramatically accelerating the exploration of parameter space. The system directly reveals dynamic phase evolution and metastable states, providing a more robust and efficient platform for establishing the fundamental strain-scaling relationship.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Contact Networks to Model Percolation in Nanoscale Phase-Separated Conductors
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from epidemiology and network science. The analogous problem is determining the epidemic threshold for disease spread in a population, which is solved by modeling the population as a contact network and applying percolation theory to find the critical transmission probability. We adapt this solution to the context of charge transport in homogeneous nanoscale phase-separated systems. Here, the conductive domains are analogous to 'infected' individuals, and the charge transport pathways are analogous to disease transmission contacts. By constructing a network model from nanoscale morphological data (e.g., from electron microscopy) and applying adapted epidemiological percolation models, we can quantify the precise percolation threshold—the critical combination of domain size, volume fraction, and inter-domain tunneling probability—required for the emergence of long-range conductivity. This approach provides a rigorous, predictive framework to model and measure percolation conditions by directly translating tools from network epidemiology to materials science.

ideas.title: Adapt Cryo-Electron Tomography Workflows for Correlative Nanoscale Phase Domain Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET) in structural biology to the challenge of imaging nanoscale phase domains. In cryo-ET, the analogous problem of imaging fragile, hydrated biological structures in 3D is solved by a workflow of rapid vitrification to preserve native state, followed by tilt-series imaging in an electron microscope and computational 3D reconstruction. We adapt this solution by applying high-resolution scanning probe microscopy (SPM) techniques from semiconductor physics within a similar workflow: first, rapidly vitrifying soft matter (e.g., block copolymer, perovskite precursor, or battery electrode) samples to arrest dynamic phase domains; second, using a cryo-compatible SPM to acquire nanoscale property maps (e.g., conductivity, stiffness, surface potential) at multiple 'tilt' angles or in a 3D scanning mode; and third, employing computational tomography algorithms to reconstruct the three-dimensional morphology and property distribution of the phase-separated nanostructures, revealing buried interfaces and domain connectivity crucial for material function.

ideas.title: Strain-Tuned Phase Domain Patterning via Epitaxial Template Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from polymer physics, where external templates control the periodicity of block copolymer self-assembly, to the problem of scaling phase domain size in complex oxide or semiconductor thin films. The analogous solution uses directed self-assembly on pre-patterned substrates to set a non-intrinsic length scale. Adapted to epitaxial strain systems, the research idea proposes to design and fabricate epitaxial substrates with a deliberate, non-uniform strain landscape—such as using lateral superlattices of alternating materials, compositionally graded buffer layers, or substrates with engineered topological features (e.g., nanoscale pillars or trenches). When the target thin film is grown epitaxially on such a substrate, the spatially modulated strain field imposed by the template acts as an elastic potential that biases the phase separation process. This strain template energetically penalizes phase domain sizes that are incommensurate with its own periodicity, thereby 'directing' the assembly to adopt a characteristic length scale set by the designer substrate pattern, rather than solely by the film's intrinsic thermodynamic parameters. This method provides a deterministic, external knob to scale the phase domain size via engineered epitaxial strain gradients.

ideas.title: Correlative Environmental SPM for Nanoscale Phase Domain Dynamics
ideas.description: This redesigned approach applies a modular scanning probe microscopy system, engineered specifically for complex materials. It integrates high-speed atomic force microscopy for structural imaging with simultaneous electrical (SSRM, KPFM) and mechanical mapping, all within a precisely controlled environmental cell. This allows for the direct observation of nanoscale phase domain formation, evolution, and property variation under realistic, non-ultrahigh vacuum conditions—such as in hydrated biomaterials or operating battery interfaces. The system overcomes the sample compatibility flaw by adapting the measurement to the material's native state, not vice versa, enabling mechanistic studies of phase dynamics in functional materials.

ideas.title: Field-Guided Hierarchical Processing for Macroscopic Nanocomposites with Long-Range Phase Order
ideas.description: This research designs a manufacturing process that uses dynamic external fields to direct and correct the assembly of nanoscale phase-separated domains over macroscopic scales. Instead of relying on static self-assembly, the system applies sequenced shear, electric, or magnetic fields during material solidification to actively align domains and annihilate defects as they form. This approach overcomes the kinetic barriers and instability that limit order in large volumes, enabling the production of bulk nanocomposite samples with coherent, long-range periodic nanostructure. The goal is to translate the exceptional properties of ordered nanocomposites from thin-film prototypes to practical, scalable materials.

ideas.title: Adapting Block Copolymer Directed Self-Assembly for Long-Range Ordered Nanocomposites
ideas.description: This idea adapts the scalable 'directed self-assembly' (DSA) solution from block copolymer lithography in semiconductor manufacturing to engineer nanocomposites with long-range ordered nanoscale domains. In the analogous field, a coarse pre-pattern guides the self-assembly of block copolymers, aligning their nanoscale microphase separation over entire wafers. Adapted here, a nanocomposite material system (e.g., metal oxide in a polymer matrix, or two immiscible ceramic phases) is deposited at scale. A master template with micron-scale features—created via scalable techniques like nanoimprint lithography or laser interference patterning—is then used to direct the phase separation during a subsequent annealing or reaction step. This template imposes a registry, guiding the nucleation and coalescence of the nanodomains into a single, defect-minimized, long-range ordered superstructure within the bulk composite, translating local self-assembly into global order.
